US Macro Regime State, Momentum and Transition Risk
Home
US Macro Regime State, Momentum and Transition Risk
An integrated view of regime conditions, momentum shifts, structural tensions, and transition risks

Macro Regime Assessment:

  • The macro environment is characterized by a fragile expansionary growth phase.
  • Stability is fragile and momentum is decelerating as fundamental support narrows.
  • Rising tensions between restrictive real interest rates and liquidity expansion shape the backdrop.
  • The primary macro risk is a transition toward a late-cycle slowdown.
  • Asset posture is neutral for equities, flattening for rates, and risk-off for the US dollar.
  • Regime confidence is moderate as cross-theme alignment has decreased significantly.

Regime Classification: Expansionary / Risk-Positive

Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence

Why This Regime:

  • High-weight cyclical leads in housing and capital expenditure support the growth trend (1).
  • Speculative intensity in global trade and physical market shortages reinforce the expansion (2).
  • Healthy private sector credit growth and easing bank availability provide a robust cyclical anchor (3).
  • Signals for labor and liquidity were downweighted due to a lack of high aggregation priority (4)(5).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Supportive drivers align across domestic growth, credit availability, and global trade demand (1)(2)(3).
  • Restrictive real yields conflict with rising M2 money supply and liquidity growth (4).
  • Headline labor stability is challenged by rising underemployment and manufacturing hours contraction (5).
  • Tensions do not yet overturn the regime call due to resilient housing and credit leads.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant Scenario: Continued expansion driven by equipment demand and residential construction (1).
  • Primary Upside Risk: A blow-off top driven by accelerating speculative crowding in trade (2).
  • Primary Downside Risk: Acceleration of underemployment stress leading to cyclical contraction (5).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A 3-month housing composite momentum reversal falling below the -0.60 threshold (1).
  • The Private Credit Impulse composite falling below 0.75 for two consecutive periods (3).
  • Ten-year real yields falling below 1.915% alongside weakening M2 growth (4).

Model Lineage

Visualisations below present upstream signal states and evidence context used to form the current silver market interpretation.

Macro Regime Assessment

Regime Assessment • Layer 1

The US macro regime remains in an expansionary phase, primarily driven by robust cyclical leads in housing, capital expenditure intentions, and healthy private sector credit growth. This core strength is reinforced by high speculative intensity in global trade and physical market shortages in energy and metals. However, this expansion is exceptionally fragile, as evidenced by restrictive real interest rates and a deteriorating labor market backdrop. While institutional dip-buying persists in growth sectors, it faces significant friction from a stressed volatility environment and tightening global liquidity. The divergence between strong real-sector indicators and weakening internal stability suggests that while the growth trend persists, the margin for error has narrowed significantly. Investors should monitor real yields and labor market turnover, as these structural tensions pose the greatest risk to the current expansionary trajectory.

Dominant Drivers

  • macro_theme_us_growth_business_cycle (Risk-Positive)
  • macro_theme_global_trade_demand (Risk-on)
  • macro_theme_us_credit_conditions (Risk-Positive)

Key Tensions

  • Restrictive real yields (2.02%) vs rising liquidity growth and M2 expansion
  • Headline labor stability vs rising underemployment and manufacturing hours contraction
  • Institutional dip-buying in growth sectors vs eroding price action and volatility stress

Primary Risks

  • Forced systemic de-leveraging if positioning breadth reaches elevated alert levels
  • Treasury General Account replenishment collapsing bank reserves faster than liquidity can drain
  • Upstream cost-push pressure triggering a hot inflation regime shift

Scenario Balance

  • Base Case: Continued cyclical expansion driven by equipment demand and residential construction
  • Alternative 1: Transition to Late-Cycle Growth if speculative crowding leads to a blow-off top
  • Alternative 2: Shift to Disinflationary Slowdown if real-sector orders weaken abruptly

What Would Change the Regime

  • A 3-month housing composite momentum reversal falling below -0.60
  • Decline in the Private Credit Impulse composite below 0.75 for two consecutive periods
  • 10-year real yield falling below 1.915%
  • High Yield spreads moving above 3.67%

Regime Change • Layer 1

Over the last nine days, the macro environment has transitioned to an Expansionary Growth regime, yet this shift is marked by a severe narrowing of fundamental support. While the Regime Confidence Index remains flat at 67, the alignment ratio has dropped sharply from 0.78 to 0.33, with supporting themes decreasing by four. This suggests the current expansion relies on a dwindling number of cyclical drivers while facing rising tensions from restrictive real yields and deteriorating labor market internals. Because the breadth of support is narrowing and internal frictions are intensifying, momentum is classified as decelerating, significantly increasing the risk of a regime transition.

Regime Phase & Transition Risk • Layer 2

The current expansionary growth regime has entered a late-cycle phase, characterized by a significant narrowing of fundamental support and rising internal friction. While the headline growth trend persists, the underlying stability is fragile as restrictive interest rates and deteriorating labor market conditions begin to weigh on the cycle. Momentum is decelerating, with the number of supporting themes dropping sharply over the past week. The primary risk to this regime is two-sided: persistent upstream price pressures may prevent necessary policy easing, while any further weakening in employment could trigger a rapid transition toward a slowdown. Investors should prepare for increased volatility as the gap between resilient headline data and weakening internal signals widens, suggesting the margin for error in the current expansion has become uncomfortably thin.

    Phase: Late
    Phase Confidence: Medium

Policy Surprise Risk

Direction: Two-sided

  • Real yields at 2.02% are restrictive and exceed the six-month trend
  • Upstream cost-push pressure conflicts with disinflationary PCE signals
  • Labor underemployment stress is rising despite headline stability
  • USD strength is tightening global liquidity conditions

Momentum Evidence

  • Alignment ratio dropped from 0.78 to 0.33 in nine days
  • Supporting themes decreased from seven to three
  • Tension trend is rising across labor and liquidity themes
  • Transition risk direction is increasing as breadth narrows

Tension Summary

  • Restrictive real yields vs rising liquidity growth
  • Headline labor stability vs rising underemployment and manufacturing hours contraction
  • Institutional dip-buying in growth sectors vs eroding price action and volatility stress

What to Watch Next

  • 10-year real yield falling below 1.915%
  • Private Credit Impulse composite falling below 0.75
  • Underemployment composite z-score falling below +0.75
  • High Yield spreads moving above 3.67%
  • Housing composite momentum reversal below -0.60
  • USD 4-week positioning flow turning negative
  • Energy sector hedger long share falling below 20%

Asset Class Implications • Layer 3

The late-cycle expansionary regime exhibits decelerating momentum and narrowing fundamental support, warranting a transition toward more balanced and defensive asset-class postures.

Postures reflect a late-cycle phase where decelerating momentum and fragile stability increase the risk of a regime transition. Rising transition risk and narrowing alignment between growth drivers and market internals necessitate a shift away from pure pro-risk orientations.

Regime Metrics

    Assessment: Expansionary Growth
    Confidence: Medium
    Alignment Ratio: 0.3333333333333333 (Total: 9, Supporting: 3, Mixed: 6, Conflicting: 0)
    Lookback (days): 9
    Transition Risk: Increasing

Confidence & Momentum

    RCI Change: 0
    RCI Momentum: Flat
    Momentum Signal: Decelerating

Stability & Tensions

    Stability Change: No change
    Tension Trend: Rising
    Transition Direction: Increasing

Alignment Dynamics

    Alignment Change: -0.4444444444444445
    Alignment Trend: Narrowing
    Supporting Themes Δ: -4
    Conflicting Themes Δ: 0

Asset-Class Implications

    Equities: Neutral
    Rates: Flattening
    Commodities: Mixed
    USD: Risk-Off
Cross Asset Positioning & Sentiment Analyst

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • The primary VIX driver remains in a STRESSED regime despite stabilizing momentum (1).
  • High-weight Russell 2000 positioning confirms persistent de-risking and long reduction (2).
  • Large-cap indices (Nasdaq, S&P) show stabilizing long builds during price weakness (3)(4).
  • Portfolio stability has deteriorated to the extreme upper boundary of the "Normal" classification (5).
  • Hedger Pressure is downweighted due to internal conflict and mixed signal interpretation (6).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • VIX stress aligns with persistent speculative outflows in the Russell 2000 (1)(2).
  • Tension exists between eroding price action and institutional dip-buying in Growth sectors (3)(4).
  • Fed Funds flow is stabilizing, suggesting improved alignment with policy expectations (7).
  • Tactical squeeze and exhaustion breadths are normal, supporting a shift away from earlier extremes (8).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Persistent volatility with defensive institutional rotation.
  • Primary upside risk: A sharp growth-equity relief rally triggered by short-covering (3).
  • Primary downside risk: Forced systemic de-leveraging if positioning breadth reaches elevated alert levels (5).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • The Early Warning Index crossing above the 0.50 threshold into an Elevated Risk state (5).
  • VIX falling below 16 alongside a negative MVS z-score (1).
  • Agricultural Speculative Crowding share dropping below the 0.40 threshold (9).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
US Growth & Business Cycle

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Expansionary / Risk-Positive
  • Regime Confidence Index: High Confidence
  • Status: Stable

Why This Regime:

  • High-weight primary drivers indicate robust cyclical expansion across housing and business investment (1)(2).
  • Monetary conditions reinforce this growth through an accommodative real policy stance (3).
  • Dynamic weighting prioritized High-Confidence, High-Weight signals over Indeterminate data.
  • Durable goods orders were downweighted due to critical data gaps and low interpretation confidence (4).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Housing starts and capital expenditure intent show strong positive alignment, signaling broad-based cyclical momentum (1)(2).
  • Steepening yield curve trends support a transition toward a growth-oriented macro environment (5).
  • Tension exists in industrial production, which remains neutral as volume growth lags leading indicators (6).
  • The yield curve slope remains in a flat/neutral zone, awaiting a confirmed breakout to expansionary levels (7).
  • Tensions are viewed as lagging characteristics of an early-to-mid expansion phase.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Continued cyclical expansion driven by equipment demand and residential construction.
  • Primary upside risk: Sharp decline in mortgage rates accelerating the housing pipeline (1).
  • Primary downside risk: Real-sector contraction (YoY < 0%) if forward orders weaken abruptly (6).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A 3-month housing composite momentum reversal falling below -0.60 (1).
  • Closing of the accommodative stance gap above the -0.50 threshold (3).
  • Capex intent composite falling below the 0.75 expansion threshold (2).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
US Inflation and Price Dynamics

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • The primary drivers are four medium-weighted signals (PCE, Term-Structure, Market-Implied, and CPI/PPI Divergence) currently holding in stable or neutral bands (1)(2)(3)(4).
  • Dynamic weighting prioritizes high-confidence PCE and Market-Implied signals, which indicate range-bound price paths (1)(3).
  • The US Inflation Signal was downweighted due to internal conflict and mixed interpretation confidence (5).
  • Wage Growth was downweighted due to low interpretation confidence (6).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Market-implied expectations and the PCE composite align in a persistent neutral state (1)(3).
  • Tension exists between the US Inflation Signal’s rapid decline toward disinflation (5) and the CPI/PPI Divergence signal’s rising upstream cost pressure (4).
  • Tensions do not overturn the regime because medium-weight cyclical signals remain centered within historical noise thresholds (2)(3).

Scenario Balance:

  • The dominant scenario is range-bound inflation pricing with normal volatility.
  • Primary upside risk: Upstream cost-push pressure triggers a "Hot" regime shift as PPI acceleration continues (4).
  • Primary downside risk: A confirmed disinflationary trend if the US Inflation Signal drops below -0.50 (5).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • The PCE Composite value exceeding the 0.75 threshold for a "Hot" regime transition (1).
  • The Market-Implied Inflation z-score reaching or exceeding 1.0 (3).
  • A reversal in Inflation Signal momentum exceeding +0.25 from its current level (5).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
US Liquidity and Monetary Conditions

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • The assessment is driven by a conflict between high-weight cyclical signals. 10-year real interest rates remain in a restrictive Bearish regime (1). This headwind is countered by a Neutral but improving Federal Reserve Liquidity Composite (2) and a newly Bullish M2 money supply signal (3).
  • Dynamic weighting prioritizes the High Weight/High Confidence restrictive rate trend (1) over the Medium Weight expansion in M2 (3).
  • The SOF 5Y CoT signal was downweighted due to Mixed Interpretation Confidence and Low Aggregation Weight (4).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Internal alignment exists between broad liquidity metrics; the Fed composite (2) and M2 momentum (3) both reflect expansionary bank reserves and credit creation.
  • Tension is centered on the real cost of capital. High real yields (2.02%) currently exceed the six-month trend (1), creating restrictive financial conditions that oppose liquidity growth.
  • This tension prevents a full Expansionary label, as restrictive borrowing costs typically precede growth-sensitive asset pressure.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Range-bound persistence as neutral policy positioning (5) absorbs the friction between high rates and rising liquidity.
  • Primary upside risk: A move to Expansionary if real yields fall below 1.915% while M2 growth persists (1)(3).
  • Primary downside risk: Stress escalation if the Treasury General Account (TGA) replenishes faster than RRP can drain, collapsing bank reserves (2).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • The real interest rate trend transitions to Neutral if the 10-year real yield falls below 1.915% (1).
  • The liquidity environment shifts to Defensive if the Liquidity Composite falls below the -0.75 threshold (2).
  • Monetary stagnation is confirmed if M2 YoY growth drops below 2.0% (3).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
US Credit Conditions

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Expansionary / Risk-Positive
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • Status: Fragile Balance

Why This Regime:

  • The primary driver is the accelerating private sector credit growth, which signals a transition to a risk-on financing environment (1).
  • Corporate funding remains stable with credit spreads holding in a normal, range-bound regime (2).
  • Dynamic weighting prioritized these High-Weight, High-Confidence signals over lower-weight tactical indicators.
  • The Financial Stress Index (3) and Stress Composite (4) were downweighted due to Low Confidence ratings and missing data sub-components.

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Alignment exists between accelerating credit creation (1) and stable High Yield spreads (2), supporting cyclical expansion.
  • A significant tension is identified in equity volatility, where a spike in VIX momentum conflicts with stable credit spreads (5).
  • This tension does not overturn the regime because financing costs have not yet restricted liquidity or triggered a regime shift in core credit markers (5).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant Scenario: Sustained cyclical expansion driven by easing bank credit availability and healthy corporate demand.
  • Primary Upside Risk: Further compression in credit spreads leading to an "Easy" regime if benchmark risk allocations fall (2).
  • Primary Downside Risk: A sentiment-led shock where credit spreads widen to "catch up" with rising equity volatility (5).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A decline in the Private Credit Impulse composite below 0.75 for two consecutive periods (1).
  • A move in High Yield spreads above 3.67%, representing a 50bps widening from current levels (2).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
US Labour Market

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • High-weight structural signals present a conflict between headline stability and rising internal stress.
  • Employment Composition remains neutral based on stable participation and unemployment rates (1).
  • Underemployment Risk has shifted to a high-stress regime, driven by rising job-finding duration (2).
  • Labor Market Turnover confirms a bearish loosening as layoffs rise and quits fall (3).
  • Signals for Slack Gap (4) and Sectoral Diffusion (5) were downweighted due to low interpretation confidence and internal conflicts.

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Negative Net Job Creation (6) aligns with bearish Consumer Sentiment (7) to indicate demand headwinds.
  • Improving jobless claims momentum (8) creates tension with manufacturing hours contraction (9).
  • Tensions do not overturn the regime because high-weight labor underutilization (2) and turnover trends (3) suggest cyclical deterioration despite headline neutrality (10).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant Scenario: Continued fragility as underlying labor slack offsets nominal employment stability.
  • Primary Upside Risk: Transition to tightening triggered by a recovery in payroll momentum (8).
  • Primary Downside Risk: Acceleration of underemployment stress leading to a broader cyclical contraction (2).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A move in the Employment Composition z-score beyond +/- 0.75 (1).
  • The Underemployment composite z-score falling below the +0.75 stress threshold (2).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
Global Growth and Economic Cycle

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • Status: The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • The primary cyclical anchor has transitioned from a demand tailwind to a neutral state as idiosyncratic copper momentum fades (1).
  • High-weight tactical signals indicate extreme fragility in battery metals, characterized by persistent crowded shorts and high positioning tension (2).
  • Dynamic weighting downweighted Crude Oil due to internal conflict and low interpretation confidence (3). Economic cycle proxies were also downweighted due to mixed signals and low trend confirmation (4).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Alignment: Copper and the energy-adjusted cyclical signal both confirm a waning demand impulse as speculative conviction resets toward neutral (5)(1).
  • Tensions: Precious metals maintain a constructive trend-long bias with positive speculative builds, contrasting with the normalization seen in base metals (6)(7).
  • Resolution: High-weight cyclical and industrial signals define the regime, as tactical precious metal flows are insufficient to override broader demand normalization.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant Scenario: Sideways consolidation as idiosyncratic demand premiums erode and positioning resets to macro-driven levels (1).
  • Primary Upside Risk: A short-covering squeeze in lithium or palladium triggered by positioning exhaustion and extreme negative z-scores (2)(8).
  • Primary Downside Risk: A broader "Spec_Long_Unwind" if speculative sponsorship in gold or silver fails to sustain recent gains (6)(9).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A shift in the energy-adjusted residual z-score beyond the neutral band (-0.75 to +0.75) would signal a return to trend-driven demand or a cyclical headwind (1).
  • Gold 4-week flow turning negative, signaling a "Long_Reduction" state and a loss of trend sponsorship (6).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
Global Inflation and FX Dynamics

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Medium Confidence
  • The regime is fragile.

Why This Regime:

  • The primary driver is a high-weight surge in USD long flow momentum (5).
  • Significant capital withdrawal is evident in CAD sponsorship and active JPY short builds (2)(6).
  • EURO and USD Index signals were downweighted due to low interpretation confidence and internal methodology conflicts (3)(7).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • USD long accumulation aligns with heavy flow contraction in commodity-linked currencies like CAD (5)(2).
  • Extreme crowding in AUD longs creates high fragility and drawdown risk despite positive momentum (1).
  • Tensions exist as EURO de-risking reaches extreme z-scores while spot prices diverge from positioning (3).
  • These tensions signal fragility rather than a regime shift because USD momentum remains expansionary (5).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Continued USD strength and tightening global liquidity regimes (5).
  • Primary upside risk: A violent short squeeze in JPY or EURO if global risk appetite shocks occur (6)(3).
  • Primary downside risk: Rapid liquidation of AUD longs if China macro indicators or growth cycles falter (1).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A reversal in USD 4-week positioning flow to negative values (5).
  • Multi-week flow reversals in AUD signaling a growth-cycle inflection point (1).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
Global Trade and Demand

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Late-Cycle / Crowded.
  • Regime Confidence Index: High Confidence.
  • The regime is currently stable with strengthening speculative intensity.

Why This Regime:

  • Extreme speculative futures positioning and market breadth drive the risk-on posture (1).
  • Physical market shortages in energy and metals sectors reinforce supply-side stress (2).
  • Primary drivers were weighted heavily due to high interpretation confidence and structural/cyclical relevance (1)(2).
  • The USD Index signal was downweighted due to internal methodology conflict and low conviction (3).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Aggressive risk exposure aligns with high consumer hedging against physical scarcity (1)(2).
  • Rising speculative momentum in agriculture creates a tailwind for price persistence (2).
  • Tension exists in the USD Index, where weak momentum conflicts with a nascent bullish level shift (3).
  • This tension does not overturn the regime due to the dollar signal's low aggregation weight (3).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Continued pro-cyclical asset appreciation supported by speculative appetite.
  • Primary upside risk: A blow-off top driven by accelerating speculative crowding.
  • Primary downside risk: Rapid long liquidation triggered by macro shocks or physical supply exhaustion.

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A decline in the CoT Market Tone Z-score below the 1.0 threshold (1).
  • Energy sector hedger deep long share falling below the 20% scarcity threshold (2).
IA Macro Analyst Drill-Through
